{"step_by_step_thinking": "The patient is a 3-week-old male with an inward turning of the left forefoot, which is described as a concavity of the medial border of the foot and a convex lateral border. The heel is in neutral position, and tickling the lateral border leads to correction of the deformity, indicating a flexible deformity. The x-ray shows an increased angle between the 1st and 2nd metatarsal bones, which is consistent with metatarsus adductus. This condition is commonly caused by intrauterine positioning and is typically self-correcting in infants. The presence of a skin crease just below the ball of the great toe is a classic finding in metatarsus adductus. The deformity is flexible, as it corrects with passive manipulation, which is a key feature distinguishing it from rigid deformities. The patient has no other abnormalities, and the pregnancy was complicated by oligohydramnios, which may have contributed to the intrauterine positioning but does not indicate a more serious underlying condition. According to the literature, metatarsus adductus in infants is usually a benign, self-resolving condition that requires no intervention. The most appropriate next step is reassurance, as the deformity is expected to resolve spontaneously with time. Interventions such as foot abduction braces, arthrodesis, or tarsometatarsal capsulotomy are not indicated in this age group or for this type of flexible deformity. Therefore, reassurance is the most appropriate management.", "answer_choice": "C"}
